lead: Removals can look deceptively simple from the outside: a van turns up, boxes go in, the van drives off. Spend a day alongside one of our crews, though, and you will see it is a craft built on early starts, careful judgement and a good deal of teamwork. As a family-run firm in Westerfield near Ipswich, working across Suffolk since 2005, we thought we would walk you through a typical move day from the crew's point of view, hour by hour.

sections.h: An Early Start at the Westerfield Yard
sections.body:
A move day begins long before anyone reaches your front door. The crew gather at our Westerfield base while the kettle is still warm, and the first job is to look over the day's run sheet together. Who is on the team, which property is first, how many rooms, what access to expect and whether anything unusual is involved, like a piano or a long-distance leg out of the county. A few minutes spent here saves a great deal of confusion later.

Next comes the vehicle. We check the van over properly, the tyres, the lights, the fuel, and make sure it is loaded with everything the day will need: blankets, straps, trolleys, boxes, tape and plenty of packing paper. Running out of materials halfway through a move is the kind of avoidable problem that experience teaches you to design out, so we always carry more than we think we will use rather than less.

There is also a quieter benefit to this early gathering, and it is one we value as a family business. The crew talk through how they will work as a team, who leads on the heavy items, who takes charge of labelling and loading order. Moving is physical, but it is also coordination, and a team that has set off on the same page works faster, more safely and with far less fuss when the pressure is on.

sections.h: Arriving and Sizing Up the Job
sections.body:
When we pull up outside your home, the first thing the crew do is not start carrying boxes; it is to take stock. We introduce ourselves, agree where to park the van for the easiest carry, and walk through the house with you so everyone knows what is going and what is staying. It is surprising how often a quick walk-through reveals something the survey could not, and that is exactly when we want to find it.

We are reading the property as we go. Where are the tight corners, the low beams, the awkward turn at the top of the stairs? Which is the wardrobe that will need taking apart, and where is the box of fragile kitchen things that needs the gentlest handling? Suffolk homes range from new estates to old cottages with doorways built for smaller furniture, and each one asks for a slightly different plan of attack.

This is also when we protect the home before any lifting begins. Floors get covered, doorframes and bannisters get padded, and on a wet morning we take extra care that nothing damp is walked through the house. None of this takes long, but it sets the tone for a careful day. Only once we have a clear plan and the house is protected does the real work of moving start in earnest.

sections.h: Wrapping, Lifting and the Rhythm of the Morning
sections.body:
With the plan agreed, the crew settle into a rhythm, and watching a practised team at work is genuinely satisfying. We tend to work room by room, clearing one space completely before moving on, so the house empties in an orderly way rather than dissolving into chaos. Furniture is wrapped where it stands, boxes are checked and labelled, and a steady stream of items begins making its way out to the van.

Lifting is where technique matters far more than brute strength, and it is a big part of how our crews stay fit to do this job year after year. We lift with our legs, keep loads close to the body, and never hesitate to make something a two-person job rather than risk a back or a bruised doorframe. Trolleys and straps do the hard work on the heavy pieces, turning a wardrobe that two people would struggle with into a manageable, controlled move.

Communication runs through all of it. A move grinds to a halt without it, so the crew keep up a constant, easy patter: who is coming through the door, which way a sofa needs to turn, where a heavy box is being set down. We have found over the years that the friendly, talkative crews are also the safest ones, because everyone always knows what everyone else is doing. The morning passes quickly when the team is working in step.

sections.h: Loading the Van and the Lunchtime Reset
sections.body:
As items come out of the house, one of the crew takes charge of the van, and loading well is a skill in its own right. Heavy, sturdy furniture goes in first to build a stable base, then boxes and lighter pieces are tiered and wedged so there are no gaps for anything to slide into. Fragile boxes are kept clear of anything that could crush them, and we deliberately load so that whatever is needed first at the new house comes off the van last.

It is a bit like a puzzle that has to be solved against the clock, and an experienced loader is always thinking a few items ahead, picturing how the whole load will lock together. Straps secure the larger pieces, blankets sit between surfaces that might rub, and by the time the doors close the contents of an entire home have become one solid, settled block ready for the road.

Somewhere around the middle of the day, the crew take a proper break, and this is not laziness; it is good sense. A short pause to eat and rest keeps everyone sharp and safe through the afternoon, because tired movers make mistakes and mistakes are how things get dropped. We also use the moment to check the plan for the unload, confirm timings with you and make sure nothing has been left behind in a cupboard or loft.

sections.h: On the Road and Into the New Home
sections.body:
The drive to the new house is the calmest part of the day for the customer, but the crew are still working. We pick sensible routes for a loaded van, keep speeds gentle and corners smooth so the load stays settled, and stay in touch about timings so you are never left wondering when we will arrive. For a longer move out across the county we plan the journey carefully and check the load is still secure along the way.

Arriving at the new property, the routine mirrors the morning in reverse. Before anything comes off the van the crew walk the new home, note the layout and the tricky corners, and protect floors and doorways just as we did at the old house. Then the unload begins, and because we loaded with this moment in mind, boxes and furniture go straight to the right room rather than piling up in the hall for you to sort out later.

This is where labelling earns its keep. A box marked for the kitchen goes to the kitchen, a bed goes up to the right bedroom, and the things you need first are easy to reach rather than buried. The crew reassemble beds, wardrobes and tables, and set the heavy pieces where you actually want them. Throughout the move your belongings are fully covered while they are in our hands, which lets everyone get on with the job calmly.

sections.h: The Final Walk-Round and the Trip Home
sections.body:
When the last item is in place, the crew do not simply vanish. We take a walk round with you to check everything has come off the van, that nothing has been overlooked, and that you are happy with where the furniture has ended up. If a wardrobe would be better against the other wall or a sofa turned around, now is the moment to say so, while there are still willing hands to shift it. As a local family firm, we would much rather get that right than drive off too soon.

We also gather up our blankets, straps and any leftover materials, leaving your new home tidy rather than strewn with packing debris. A good move should end with you standing in a home you can actually start living in, not a maze of boxes and a floor full of wrapping. That final tidy-up is a small thing, but it is part of how we like to finish every single job.

Then it is back to Westerfield, where the day is still not quite done. The van is cleaned and checked over, the kit is sorted ready for tomorrow, and the team have a quick word about how the move went and whether anything is worth remembering for next time. It is this steady, end-to-end care, repeated move after move since 2005, that turns a hard day's work into the smooth experience our Suffolk customers remember long after the boxes are unpacked.

faqs: What time will the crew arrive on moving day?

We agree a start time with you in advance and aim to arrive promptly, having already gathered and checked the van at our Westerfield yard first thing. The exact time depends on the size of the move and the distance involved. We will confirm timings beforehand and keep in touch on the day, so you are never left guessing where we are.
How many people will be in the removals crew?

It depends on the size of your home and the access at both ends. A small flat might need a two-person team, while a larger family house with stairs and heavy furniture calls for more hands. We work this out during the survey so the right number of crew turn up, which keeps the day safe, steady and on schedule.
Do the crew take a break during the move?

Yes, and it matters. A short, sensible break around the middle of the day keeps everyone sharp and safe through the afternoon, because tired movers are when mistakes and breakages happen. We use the pause to refuel, confirm the plan for the unload and double-check nothing has been left behind in a cupboard or loft.
What does the crew do when they reach the new house?

Before unloading they walk the property, note the layout and protect the floors and doorways. Then they unload in order, taking each box and piece of furniture straight to the room marked on the label, reassembling beds and wardrobes and placing heavy items where you want them. A final walk-round makes sure you are happy before they leave.
